# LLM-Assisted Engineering Estimation Methodology
<!-- Generated By: Claude Code (Claude Opus 4.6) -->

## TL;DR

Traditional estimation assumes humans write code. In spec-driven AI development (spec-kit, Claude Code, CLAUDE.md), humans write specifications and AI writes implementation — the bottleneck shifts from coding to context preparation, verification, and non-code work. This document presents a citation-backed methodology for estimating work in this model, synthesized from 84 sources including 8 RCTs, 17 industry reports, and analysis from Google, Microsoft, Anthropic, and independent researchers.

**The target workflow:** Specify -> Plan -> AI Implements -> Human Verifies. AI writes most or all implementation code (80-100%), not the industry average of 42% [28].

**The core insight:** Even when AI writes all the code, coding is only 25-35% of delivery [38]. The estimation challenge is not "how fast can AI code?" but "how much time do specification, verification, and non-code work take?"

**The critical variable:** Without structured specs, AI makes experienced developers 19% *slower* [3]. With proper specs, AI achieves 95% first-attempt accuracy [57] and 21-56% coding speedup [1][4]. The 40-75 percentage point swing is entirely determined by context engineering quality. Self-reported velocity gains are unreliable estimation inputs — developers overestimate AI's benefit by 39-44 pp [3].

---

## 1. Why Traditional Estimation Breaks

Traditional estimation (story points, ideal days, COCOMO) assumes:
- Humans write all code
- Implementation is the primary time sink
- Review is a fixed fraction of implementation
- Quality is a function of developer skill

With spec-driven AI development, all four assumptions fail:

| Assumption | Reality in Spec-Driven AI | Evidence |
|---|---|---|
| Humans write all code | AI writes 80-100% of implementation code (industry avg: 42% [28]) | Spec-kit workflow; Sonar 2026 survey |
| Implementation is the bottleneck | Spec writing + verification replace coding as the time sinks | [38][41][47] |
| Review is a fixed fraction | Review expands: more code to verify, review against spec contract | Faros [27]: +91% review time industry-wide |
| Quality tracks developer skill | AI code has 1.7x more issues [31], 2.74x more vulnerabilities [32]; spec quality becomes the quality lever | CodeRabbit, Veracode, Red Hat [57] |

### The Productivity Paradox

Six independent studies converge on the same finding: individual productivity gains from AI tools do not translate to organizational throughput improvements [27].

| Metric | Individual | Organizational | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tasks completed | +21% | No DORA improvement | [27] |
| PRs merged | +98% | Delivery throughput -1.5% | [21][27] |
| Self-reported savings | 3.6-4.4 hrs/week | Delivery stability -7.2% | [26][21] |

**Root cause:** Amdahl's Law -- accelerating one phase (coding) doesn't help when other phases (review, testing, deployment) are the constraint. AI generates code faster, then everything piles up at the next bottleneck [27].

---

## 2. The Evidence Base

### Hierarchy of Evidence Used

| Evidence Type | Count | Used For |
|---|---|---|
| Randomized Controlled Trials | 8 | Productivity multipliers, task-type effects |
| Peer-reviewed research | 10 | Defect rates, hallucination, benchmarks |
| Large-scale industry surveys | 17 | Adoption rates, time allocation, trust |
| Practitioner analysis | 12 | Frameworks, workflow models |
| Tool evaluations | 12 | Estimation tool landscape |

### Key Quantitative Anchors

These numbers appear throughout the methodology and are the most robust findings:

| Finding | Value | Source | Quality |
|---|---|---|---|
| AI makes experienced devs slower | +19% time | METR RCT [3] | High (RCT, small N) |
| Perception-reality gap | 39-44 pp | METR RCT [3] | High |
| Coding share of SDLC | 25-35% | Bain 2025 [38] | High |
| Coding share of traditional dev time | 16-39% | IDC [33], Tidelift [34] | Moderate |
| System-level ceiling (tool addition) | ~10% | Six studies [27] | High (convergent) |
| System-level ceiling (workflow transformation) | 25-30% | Bain 2025 [38] | Moderate |
| AI code defect rate | 1.7x more issues | CodeRabbit [31] | Moderate |
| Delivery stability change | -7.2% per 25% AI adoption | DORA 2024 [21] | High |
| Context miss rate | 65% of devs report it | Qodo [30] | Moderate |

---

## 3. The Spec-Driven Estimation Model

### Traditional Model

```
Total Effort = Implementation + Testing + Review + Deployment
```

### Spec-Driven AI Model

```
Total Effort = Specification + AI Implementation + Verification + Non-Code Work
```

This maps directly to the spec-kit workflow: **Constitution -> Specify -> Plan -> Tasks -> Implement -> Verify** [53].

Where each phase has distinct characteristics:

| Phase | Description | Traditional SDLC Share | Spec-Driven AI Share | Who |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Specification** | Constitution, specs, CLAUDE.md, task decomposition | 5-10% | **20-30%** | Human (orchestrator) |
| **AI Implementation** | Code generation, iteration cycles, compaction | 25-35% (manual coding) | **5-15%** (AI generates, human iterates) | AI + Human |
| **Verification** | Review against spec, testing (AI-assisted), security scan | 20-30% | **25-35%** (more code, AI-assisted test generation) | Human + AI |
| **Non-Code Work** | Meetings, deployment, documentation, stakeholder communication | 25-40% | **25-35%** (docs partially AI-assisted) | Human |

**Key shift:** Specification time grows from a minor overhead to a major phase. This is the ROI investment — 30 minutes of spec writing can prevent hours of AI-generated rework. Implementation time shrinks dramatically but verification grows because there's more code to review and comprehend.

### The Spec Quality Spectrum

| Approach | First-Attempt Accuracy | Productivity Effect | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| No specs (ad-hoc prompting) | 70-82% | -19% (slower) for experienced devs | [3][78] |
| Basic CLAUDE.md + rough spec | ~85% (est.) | Moderate improvement | [30] |
| Detailed spec (spec-kit workflow) | 95%+ | +21-56% coding speedup | [57][1][4] |

With spec-kit, Osmani's 70/30 heuristic [41] shifts closer to **90/10 or 95/5** for well-specified tasks — AI handles the vast majority correctly on first attempt, and the remaining 5-10% edge cases are identified during verification against the spec.

**Estimation implication:** The quality of the spec determines the number of iteration cycles. Budget specification time as a first-class activity, not overhead.

---

## 4. Task Suitability Classification

Before estimating, classify each task by **spec quality + task type**:

| Category | Spec Quality | Task Type | Multiplier | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A: High AI Acceleration** | Detailed spec exists | Greenfield, CRUD, boilerplate, test scaffolding | 0.50 - 0.70 of traditional | Spec-kit workflow with clear acceptance criteria |
| **B: Moderate AI Acceleration** | Basic spec / CLAUDE.md | Feature work, refactoring, API integration | 0.65 - 0.85 of traditional | CLAUDE.md exists, task is described but not fully specified |
| **C: Spec Absence Penalty** | No spec / ad-hoc prompting | Same tasks as A/B | 0.90 - 1.15 of traditional | METR-like usage: AI without structured context [3] |
| **D: Human Judgment Dominates** | Any | Architecture, complex debugging, cross-system integration | 1.0 - 1.25 of traditional | AI assists research but human drives decisions |

**Category C is the critical insight:** The same task that's Category A with a spec becomes Category C without one. The METR -19% finding [3] is a Category C result — unstructured AI use on mature codebases. Spec quality, not task type alone, determines the category.

### Classification Criteria

| Factor | Favors AI (Category A/B) | Against AI (Category C/D) |
|---|---|---|
| **Spec completeness** | Detailed spec with acceptance criteria | No spec or vague description |
| Codebase context | CLAUDE.md/AGENTS.md exists and is current | No project context files |
| Task scope | Fits in 40-60% of context window | Exceeds context window |
| Verification approach | Spec defines test criteria; tests can be generated | Requires exploratory testing or human judgment |
| Quality requirements | Standard (spec-verifiable) | Safety-critical, security-sensitive |

---

## 5. The Estimation Rubric

### Step 1: Classify the Task

Use Section 4 to assign Category A, B, C, or D.

### Step 2: Estimate Using Spec-Driven Phases

Instead of starting from a traditional estimate, estimate each phase of the spec-driven workflow:

```
Total = Spec_Time + AI_Implementation_Time + Verification_Time + Non_Code_Time
```

| Phase | How to Estimate |
|---|---|
| **Specification** | Proportional to task complexity. Simple: 15-30 min. Moderate: 1-2 hrs. Complex: 4-8 hrs. |
| **AI Implementation** | Traditional coding estimate x Task Multiplier (0.50 - 1.25 from Section 4) |
| **Verification** | 1.0x - 2.0x of AI implementation time. Review against spec + AI-assisted test generation + security scan. |
| **Non-Code** | Meetings, deployment, stakeholder work — estimate as before. AI assists documentation (0.3-0.5x) and test writing (0.5-0.8x). |

### Step 3: Apply Adjustment Factors

| Factor | Range | How to Determine |
|---|---|---|
| **Task Multiplier** | 0.50 - 1.25 | From Section 4 classification (spec quality + task type) |
| **Rework Factor** | 1.05 - 1.15 | 1.05 with spec + strong tests; 1.15 without |

### Step 4: The Delivery Composition

In a spec-driven AI workflow, the SDLC phases shift significantly from traditional proportions:

| Phase | Traditional SDLC | Spec-Driven AI SDLC | Change |
|---|---|---|---|
| Specification & context | 5-10% | **20-30%** | Grows — this is the ROI investment |
| Implementation | 25-35% | **5-15%** | Shrinks dramatically (AI generates) |
| Verification | 20-30% | **25-35%** | Grows — more code, review against spec |
| Non-code (meetings, deploy, docs) | 25-40% | **25-35%** | Slightly shrinks (AI-assisted docs/tests) |

**The Bain insight reframed [38]:** In traditional SDLC, coding is 25-35% — so coding speedups have limited total impact. In spec-driven AI SDLC, implementation shrinks to 5-15%, but specification and verification grow to fill the gap. The total delivery improvement potential is 25-30% with end-to-end workflow transformation [38], not the ~10% ceiling seen with tool-addition alone [27].

### Worked Example

**Task:** Add user authentication to a greenfield web service (spec-kit workflow)

| Phase | Estimate | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| **Specification** | 0.5 days (4 hrs) | Write auth spec: endpoints, token handling, error cases, acceptance criteria. Constitution already exists. |
| **AI Implementation** | 0.5 days | Category A (detailed spec, greenfield, well-documented auth patterns). Traditional coding estimate would be 3 days; x 0.50 task multiplier x 1.05 rework = 1.58 days, but spec-kit generates iteratively, with Claude Code implementing against spec. Realistic: half a day with 1-2 iteration cycles. |
| **Verification** | 1.0 days | Review generated code against spec. Run AI-generated tests. Security scan for auth patterns. Manual check of edge cases in spec. |
| **Non-code** | 1.5 days | Deployment pipeline config, stakeholder demo, documentation (AI-drafted from spec, human-reviewed). |
| **Total** | **3.5 days** | |

**Traditional estimate for same task:** 8-10 days (3 days coding + 2 days testing + 1 day review + 2-4 days non-code).

**Improvement:** ~56-65% faster overall — much larger than the Bain Correction's ~10% because the entire workflow changes, not just the coding tool.

---

## 6. Capacity Planning

### Sprint Capacity Formula

```
Sprint_Capacity = Team_Size x Hours_Per_Sprint x Focus_Factor x Weighted_AI_Multiplier x (1 - Rework_Factor)
```

| Parameter | Range | Source |
|---|---|---|
| Focus_Factor | 0.60 - 0.90 | Standard [sprint research] |
| Weighted_AI_Multiplier | 1.0 - 1.3 (weighted average across task mix; individual tasks may reach 1.5x per [team-sizing.md](reference/team-sizing.md)) | Section 4 |
| Rework_Factor | 0.05 - 0.15 | [27] (+9% bugs, +91% review) |

### Velocity Recalibration Protocol

1. **Week 0-4 (adoption):** Expect 2-4 week productivity *drop* during learning curve [27]
2. **Month 2-3 (stabilization):** Begin tracking new velocity. Use last 3 sprints as baseline.
3. **Month 4+ (steady state):** Apply conservative multiplier (start at 1.1x, adjust empirically)
4. **Quarterly:** Reassess multipliers as tools and team proficiency evolve

### Team Sizing Guidance

| Team Context | Recommended Size | Key Consideration |
|---|---|---|
| MVP / greenfield | 2-3 humans + AI [59] | Front-load spec writing |
| Standard product work | 4-6 humans + AI [59] | Need review capacity |
| Complex / legacy systems | 5-8 humans + AI [59] | AI overhead likely (Category C/D) |
| Large-scale enterprise | Smaller sub-teams [60] | Break 30-60 person teams into components |

---

## 7. Risk Framework

### AI-Specific Risk Categories

Nine risk categories unique to or amplified by AI-assisted development (see [risk-uncertainty.md](reference/risk-uncertainty.md) for details):

| # | Risk | Probability | Estimation Impact |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hallucination (fabricated packages/APIs) | 5-20% of recommendations [10] | Add dependency verification step |
| 2 | False confidence (perception gap) | Demonstrated in RCT [3] | Use measured velocity only |
| 3 | Non-determinism | Inherent to LLMs [79] | Widen estimate ranges |
| 4 | Context window overflow | Task-dependent [58] | Decompose large tasks |
| 5 | Capability overestimation by AI | Documented failure mode [80] | Include fallback plan to manual |
| 6 | Verification overhead | 30-50% of implementation (est.) | Budget explicitly |
| 7 | Security vulnerabilities | 45% of AI code [32] | Add security review phase |
| 8 | Quality degradation (18-Month Wall) | Progressive [82] | Schedule codebase health reviews |
| 9 | Benchmark-reality gap | Systematic [78] | Don't use benchmarks for estimation |

### Risk-Adjusted Estimation

For critical/high-risk tasks, apply wider ranges:

| Risk Level | Estimate Range (vs. point estimate) |
|---|---|
| Low risk (Category A, good specs, strong tests) | 0.8x - 1.2x |
| Medium risk (Category B/C, moderate context) | 0.6x - 1.5x |
| High risk (Category D, poor context, unfamiliar AI tools) | 0.5x - 2.0x |

---

## 8. Context Engineering as Estimation Input

Context quality is the largest controllable variable in AI-assisted estimation:

| Context Quality | Estimated Impact | Evidence |
|---|---|---|
| **Excellent** (detailed specs, CLAUDE.md, test cases, examples) | 95% first-attempt accuracy [57]; 21-56% faster | [1][4][57] |
| **Good** (CLAUDE.md, basic specs, familiar codebase) | Moderate improvement; reduce rework | [30] |
| **Poor** (no specs, no context files, ad-hoc prompting) | Category C: 19% slower for experienced devs [3]; frequent rework | [3] |

### Context Preparation ROI

The investment in context preparation pays for itself by reducing iteration cycles:

```
Unstructured AI use:    -19% productivity (METR) [3]
Spec-driven AI use:   +20% to +55% productivity [1][37]
                      ─────────────────────────────
Delta:                 40-75 percentage points
```

This delta is entirely determined by context engineering quality. A 30-minute spec can eliminate hours of AI-generated rework.

### Context Readiness Assessment

Before estimating, assess the project's context readiness:

| Factor | Score | Impact |
|---|---|---|
| CLAUDE.md / AGENTS.md exists and is current | +1 | Reduces per-task context overhead |
| Specs exist for the task | +1 | Reduces rework by 13-25% [57] |
| Codebase is well-documented / well-structured | +1 | AI-friendly codebase [52] |
| Team has 3+ months AI tool experience | +1 | Past learning curve [27] |
| Test suite with good coverage exists | +1 | Enables fast verification |

**Score interpretation:**
- 4-5: Use Category A/B multipliers confidently
- 2-3: Use Category B/C multipliers; budget extra context preparation
- 0-1: Use Category C/D multipliers; consider whether AI assistance will help at all

---

## 9. Non-Code Work in Spec-Driven Workflows

In spec-driven AI workflows, AI assists with more than just implementation code — it also generates tests, drafts documentation, and assists with code review. The adjustments differ from industry-average AI adoption:

| Activity | Industry Average (tool addition) | Spec-Driven AI Workflow | Rationale |
|---|---|---|---|
| Test writing | 1.0x - 1.2x of traditional | **0.5x - 0.8x** | AI generates tests from specs; human reviews. 87.7% valid test conditions [13] |
| Test execution / CI | Unchanged | Unchanged | Infrastructure constraint |
| Code review | 1.5x - 2.0x of traditional [27] | **1.0x - 1.5x** | Review against spec is more structured than open-ended review; spec serves as review contract |
| Documentation | 0.85x - 0.90x | **0.3x - 0.5x** | AI drafts from specs; much of the documentation IS the spec |
| Sprint ceremonies | Unchanged (~25%) | Unchanged | Inherently human |
| Deployment / release | Unchanged | Unchanged | Infrastructure/process constraint |
| Security review | 1.3x - 1.5x | **1.3x - 1.5x** | AI code has 2.74x more vulnerabilities [32]; no spec-driven shortcut |
| Stakeholder communication | Unchanged | Unchanged | Interpersonal |

**Key distinction:** The industry-average +91% review time increase [27] reflects teams doing ad-hoc AI code generation without specs. In spec-driven workflows, the spec serves as the review contract — reviewers check implementation against spec rather than trying to reverse-engineer intent from code. This is structurally more efficient.

### The Full Delivery Formula (Spec-Driven)

```
Total_Delivery = Spec_Phase
               + (Implementation x Task_Multiplier x Rework_Factor)
               + (Verification x Verification_Adjustment)
               + (Non_Code x Non_Code_Adjustment)
```

Using spec-driven SDLC proportions:
- Specification: 20-30% (human investment, front-loaded)
- Implementation: 5-15% (AI generates, dramatically compressed)
- Verification: 25-35% (review against spec, AI-assisted testing, security)
- Non-code: 25-35% (partially AI-assisted docs/tests)

---

## 10. Recalibration Protocol

### When to Recalibrate

| Trigger | Action |
|---|---|
| Quarterly (minimum) | Review actual vs estimated for past quarter |
| Major tool change (new AI tool, model upgrade) | Reset learning curve assumptions |
| Team composition change | Adjust individual multipliers |
| Consistent over/under-estimation (>3 sprints) | Adjust AI multiplier up or down by 0.1 |
| Context quality improvement (new specs, CLAUDE.md) | Upgrade context factor |

### What to Track

| Metric | Purpose | Source |
|---|---|---|
| Actual vs. estimated time by task category | Calibrate multipliers | Sprint retrospectives |
| Code churn rate (reverts within 2 weeks) | Detect AI quality issues | Git analytics / [23] |
| PR review time | Detect review bottleneck | Git analytics / [27] |
| Change failure rate | Detect delivery instability | DORA metrics / [21] |
| AI tool adoption rate | Identify underutilization | Tool analytics / [26] |

### Anti-Patterns to Watch For

1. **"It's faster so let's add scope"** -- Jevons Paradox [49]. Keep scope constant.
2. **"AI writes the tests too"** -- AI-generated tests may miss the same defects as AI-generated code [32]
3. **"We don't need as many people"** -- Review and verification need MORE capacity, not less [27]
4. **"Skip the spec, just prompt"** -- This is the single largest estimation error. Unstructured AI use is 19% slower [3]; spec-driven use is 21-56% faster [1][4]. The 40-75 pp swing dwarfs all other factors.
5. **"Trust the velocity numbers"** -- Self-reported gains overstate reality by 39-44 pp [3]

---

## 11. Methodology Limitations

### Known Limitations

1. **Small-N RCTs.** The METR study [3] (N=16) and Google RCT [4] (N=96) are the strongest evidence but have limited statistical power. Larger studies exist [2] (N=4,867) but were individually underpowered.

2. **Rapidly evolving tools.** All controlled studies used 2022-2025 tools. Current tools (Claude 4.5/4.6, GPT-5.x, Cursor with latest models) may produce different results. Multipliers should be treated as starting points for empirical calibration.

3. **Task representativeness.** Most RCTs test simple/greenfield tasks [1][4]. The methodology extrapolates to enterprise work, but this extrapolation is not empirically validated.

4. **Vendor bias.** Vendor-funded studies (GitHub [1], AWS [40], Ant Group [5]) consistently show 2-3x larger effects than independent studies (METR [3], Google [4]). This methodology weights independent studies more heavily.

5. **Organizational context.** AI acts as an amplifier of existing engineering practices [21][22]. The same methodology will produce different results in high-maturity vs. low-maturity organizations.

6. **Self-reported data.** Many data points (time allocation, satisfaction, productivity gains) are self-reported and subject to the demonstrated perception gap [3].

7. **No longitudinal validation.** This methodology has not been validated against real project outcomes. It should be treated as a structured starting point for empirical calibration, not a proven model.

8. **Spec-driven workflow assumption.** This methodology assumes spec-driven development (spec-kit, CLAUDE.md) as the target workflow. Teams doing ad-hoc prompting without specs should expect Category C results — the METR finding [3] of -19% applies to them, not the Category A/B multipliers. Most research evidence comes from non-spec-driven contexts; the spec-driven multipliers are derived from limited data ([57], [1], [4]) and should be calibrated empirically.

### What This Methodology Does NOT Do

- Predict exact delivery dates
- Replace team judgment
- Account for organizational dysfunction
- Handle novel technology domains (where AI has no training data)
- Substitute for proper project management

### What This Methodology DOES Do

- Provide evidence-based adjustment factors for AI-assisted estimation
- Identify which tasks benefit from AI and which don't
- Budget for previously invisible costs (context preparation, verification, review expansion)
- Establish a recalibration protocol that improves over time
- Surface risks unique to AI-assisted development

---
